chapters.title: A new edition of meaning
chapters.text:
Picture Europe at the dawn of the twentieth century. Vast numbers of people had stopped looking to religion for answers and started looking to science instead. The age brought bright inventions, including moving staircases, vacuum cleaners, and refrigerators, that changed daily life. The Wright brothers pulled off powered flight, and the long human dream of reaching the sky finally came true.

Even with all that progress, the sharpest engineers could not explain why humanity was here at all. Philosophers, including the nihilists, began to doubt that life held any meaning or order, and that doubt unsettled society. The wreckage of two world wars only deepened the sense that life might be pointless.

Existentialism rose in response to the hunger for meaning, and Jean-Paul Sartre's Being and Nothingness became the Bible of the movement. Although it first appeared in 1943, its exploration of self-creation and freedom still shapes modern thought.

Set aside time for the spiritual side of life. Caring for yourself also means caring for your soul.

It is impossible to cover every nuance of Sartre's six-hundred-page masterwork, but the core ideas can be walked through together. Join the expedition to find your true identity, the roots of your anxieties and limits, and most importantly, how to give your life more meaning.

The book was banned by the Vatican in 1948. It sold in underground editions that students passed hand to hand in the years after the war. Simone de Beauvoir, Sartre's longtime partner, would later extend many of the book's ideas in her own work.

chapters.title: Which comes first, the essence or the existence?
chapters.text:
Think back to the last time you were glued to a screen, leaning forward without realizing it. In those intense stretches, you may have forgotten the screen was there. But somewhere at the back of your mind, you still knew you were safe and just watching things play out.

That small moment points to two basic features of awareness.

Awareness is intentional, which means it always lands on something specific, like the show you are watching. At the same time, it carries a quiet side awareness of itself.

Be careful about the kind of mental diet you feed your awareness.

Philosophers have long agreed that human minds are always thinking about something. How they do it, though, has been argued over for centuries.

Immanuel Kant, a giant of Western philosophy, split the world into two layers. One layer was the thing-in-itself, the deep hidden nature of objects. The other was the appearance, the surface we actually meet. Kant believed the hidden nature constantly eludes us, and we only ever get to see the surface.

Roughly a century later, Edmund Husserl started a new school of thought called phenomenology, which tilted the camera a bit. He argued that we do meet things through appearances, but those appearances actually do reflect their deeper nature, even if we cannot take it all in at once. We catch glimpses of truth depending on where we stand. Picture an oak in your neighborhood. Whether its branches are heavy with summer leaves or stark against a winter sky, you still recognize it as the same tree. Husserl explained that with his idea of a higher unifying self, the part of the mind that pulls our scattered impressions of an object into a single picture.

Sartre pushed one step further. He argued that awareness does not need a higher unifying self to grasp the deep nature of things. Awareness meets existence head-on.

That idea pulled Sartre toward several conclusions about awareness. If awareness truly mirrors the deep nature of things, it cannot carry any rules or nature of its own, since those rules would tint the way we perceive the world. So awareness has to be a kind of pure activity.

If we want to figure out the essence of awareness, in plain words, what it is, we have to wait until some object flips it on. Only then does awareness wake up to that object. This breakthrough led to the famous line, "Existence precedes essence," which became the heart of existentialism.

But what does that really mean, and how can pure activity even be possible?

chapters.title: The price of freedom
chapters.text:
What kind of thing is so clear that it can reflect other things just as they are? For Sartre, concrete nothingness fit the bill. This is not some abstract emptiness that never shows up. We bump into concrete nothingness every day. A few examples will show how.

Absence: picture four women who meet regularly to play bridge. When one of them is late, the others feel her absence. Why? Because they expected her to be there. It shows why people think they lack something they never actually had. They simply hoped they had it. Those unfulfilled hopes are where disappointments start.

Regret: imagine a man named John at the racetrack. He wanted to bet on horse A, but he second-guessed himself and went with horse B instead. When horse A crossed the finish line first, John felt nothingness.

Question: for Sartre, this might be the most important form of nothingness. When we ask a question, such as "What is a bridge?", we do two things. First, we separate the bridge from everything else. Second, we step back from the bridge to become aware of it. Nothingness, then, lays the foundation for awareness.

You bring meaning into the world. The world does not bring it to you.

Awareness comes with a steep cost. To truly understand things, we have to step back from them and from ourselves. Anyone can test this by asking, "What am I?" None of the descriptions that come to mind, like profession, marital status, or nationality, can fully capture who we are, our essence. No matter how hard we try to pin down our identity through relationships or work, it always slips away. That struggle is not a reason to stop doing those things. It is a reason to notice the failure, accept it, and keep working toward a kind of unity.

That is why Sartre split being into two types.

Being-in-itself covers things. A chair is just a chair, and an object unifies cleanly with its nature. Being-for-itself covers people. We are not one with ourselves, and our essence is always in question, which keeps us searching for it. That sounds unsettling, but it is also freeing. The nothingness inside us lets us relate to ourselves and to the world around us, and it pours meaning and purpose into everything. The nothingness inside us is the source of our freedom.

chapters.title: Where self-imposed limits come from
chapters.text:
In existentialism, freedom is woven into consciousness. Because we are always aware of ourselves, we always feel free, and that feeling brings anguish. Yet most of us rarely feel that anguish. Why not?

Like nothingness, freedom has to stay open to question. Otherwise, it picks up rules and a nature of its own, and it turns into something else. As a result, we keep asking, "Are we really that free? Do we really lack an identity?" Those questions keep us from facing the anguish head-on. When questions like "Do my actions mean anything?" or "Are my principles valid?" come up, we tend to reach for distractions.

Unlimited freedom can paralyze us, but the only way to find a real path is to face that freedom directly.

The second reason we avoid anguish is bad faith, which is a kind of self-deception. Looking at the difference between knowing and believing can make this clearer. Take the simple fact that one plus one equals two. We do not just believe it, we know it, because the evidence is undeniable.

Now consider believing that someone is your friend. There may be some evidence, but it is rarely that clean. In good faith, we watch their actions, listening to what they say about us when we are not around and noticing whether they show up in tough times, before deciding. Bad faith flips the order. We decide to believe first, then go looking for just enough evidence to support what we want.

Bad faith becomes a way to convince ourselves that we have no freedom. Two clear examples show how it works.

Determinism: telling ourselves that our past defines us is a classic case of bad faith. We are never fully defined. We are a work in progress. Going through an abusive relationship in the past does not mean we cannot build a healthy bond with someone new. What matters is not the past itself, but how we read it and what we take from it.

Social role: when we reduce ourselves to a social role, a status, or a nationality, we are acting in bad faith. A server who believes their only purpose is to be excellent at their job, for example, is locking themselves into social expectations and missing the chance to find a real path, which closes off a huge range of possibilities and a deeper sense of purpose.

Overall, bad faith is not about ignoring past actions or refusing to take on a role. It is about recognizing that we are always much more than our history or our social function.

chapters.title: No person is an island
chapters.text:
Beyond self-deception, what else can get in the way of our freedom? To answer that, it helps to start with Sartre's revolutionary view of the Other.

Before Sartre, most realist and idealist philosophers saw human connections as purely external. They were more interested in how we know other people than in what it feels like to be in their presence, and they thought other people's existence did not really reach our inner consciousness. Sartre disagreed.

Why? Imagine someone peering through a keyhole in a hallway and suddenly seeing a person coming toward them. They freeze, hit by a wave of shame and embarrassment. Those feelings are proof that we are aware of our being-for-others.

Another person's presence changes how we act and feel, and that is just the surface. At a deeper level, our sense of self snaps into focus when we recognize another consciousness that is not our own. Through other people, we come to see that we live in the physical world and that we have a presence beyond our own minds. Under their gaze, we shift from being the observer to being the observed.

I am responsible for my being-for-others, but I am not the foundation of it, as Sartre once wrote.

The image we carry in other people's eyes is always a kind of synthesis. Society reads us through our behavior. In a way, the body becomes our mode of existence for others, which makes sense, since we cannot reach directly into another person's mind.

Others, though, read our behavior through their own freedom. How we appear in their eyes will always be a little different from our true nature. So when someone tells us we are not good enough, or even when they praise us, we should not take it too personally.

This role of Others gives them the power to limit our freedom, but not by blocking our goals, because we can always pick a different route or change our angle. The limit shows up because, through them, we can see ourselves in a new light. Picture a tribe of tall people living in isolation on an island. They have no sense of being tall until they meet people who are shorter.

Comparison with others can be used to explore yourself or to tear yourself down. The choice is yours.

chapters.title: Freedom comes with responsibility
chapters.text:
How can we have unlimited freedom if there are so many obstacles in the way of our goals? It is a fair question, but existentialism answers that the world has no obstacles built in. The world is just a collection of things-in-itself, and their only purpose is to exist. It is our freedom that turns them into obstacles or into tools for getting what we want. Why?

Like awareness, freedom is intentional and aimed at something specific. Freedom pours meaning into everything around it by tying it to a goal. Take a mountain. To a traveler looking for the fastest route, it is a barrier to get past. To a photographer chasing the perfect shot, that same mountain is a chance to be seized.

Sartre argues that this resistance from the world, the way things push back against our desires, is actually a condition for freedom. A world where every wish was instantly granted would not feel human. At the same time, Sartre does not deny that accidents and chance events can help or hinder us. Their unpredictability keeps our intentions open. We cannot plan for everything, but we stay free in how we choose to respond.

Pick your obstacles with care. They are the ground you grow on as a person.

With nearly unlimited freedom comes total responsibility for our lives. Our actions are the only thing that gives our existence meaning, and each choice should reflect our authentic freedom.

It makes no sense to complain, since nothing outside us has decided what we feel, what we live, or what we are. That line is from Sartre.

Many of our actions run on autopilot, but some demand real courage. What pushes us to act? Often, it is the feeling that something is missing. Picture workers who see their current workplace as their only option. Even when they know about openings with better pay and a friendlier atmosphere, they might brush them off as "not for people like us." Will they try to improve their workplace or move on? No.

Everything changes when those workers start to believe they deserve better. Suddenly, the old environment feels suffocating, and they are ready to break free. The shift will bring anguish and uncertainty, but it will change everything. Their whole worldview shifts, and so does the way they move through the world.

A small biographical note. Jean-Paul Sartre kept writing even while held as a prisoner of war in 1940 and 1941. He also turned down the Nobel Prize for Literature in 1964, on the grounds that a writer should not be turned into an institution. He kept the same position in 1960, when he turned down the Legion of Honor from the French government.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
The idea that we cannot say with certainty who we are can feel unsettling. It pulls the rug out from under the stability and certainty that many people chase. It is also freeing. We are not locked in by our environment or our past choices. The choices we make now are the ones that matter.

Existentialism also means there is no single right way to live. We each have to pick a path. Even if we avoid making decisions and just follow someone else's path, that avoidance itself becomes our choice. In other words, we are all condemned to be free, as Sartre put it.

A small thing to try.

Make a list of the biggest decisions you make in a week. What do they mean to you? What do they say about you? Invent a game and try playing it. Sartre believed that setting the rules for play is a good way to feel your own freedom. If you want to go deeper into philosophy, sign up for a dedicated Nibble course.

He also reminded us that each person is responsible for what they make of the world around them, and that even in moments of quiet despair, we still have the power to pick our next step.
